speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back a little bit, to coming into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the transport, and you were all sent on the truck. And then you went to the gates. And then you were in this big room with the bricks. And you were waiting--

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, big barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: The barracks. With how many other people, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that night, was came about 1,200 people in. Maybe even more, Because I know they put us in the camp in that block. And they put 800 people in that, in one block, so there was more. Of course, there was already people in there. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm thinking for the time when you're being tattooed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That first block we got into, there was 800 people in there. Of course, the next few days, they kind of took a few out. But there was 700 anyway, in that one block. And I don't know how many [INAUDIBLE] they had in there on both sides. They were big family blocks, this Auschwitz, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, they didn't stay 700 long. You know? It's just in the beginning, this was just night and day. This truck business, coming and picking up the dead, they throw them out there. So that it piddled down to about 500 afterwards. OK? Tell me about getting the tattoo. How  long did that process take? Waiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tattoo? Well, to me, it seemed like we were there all night. I mean, with the whole gang. We were about 3/4 of-- there were people in front of us. We were about 3/4 of it, by time we got out of there, our family. And it was almost towards morning when they brought us over to those barracks and assigned us to one of those things there. But we had already gotten picked, us girls, that we had-- four of us-- to go out to this quarantine block. We couldn't go with them. They put us in the quarantine block. And they went to my mom and dad, the children went in that Block 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was hoping you'd show us your number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure will. I don't know if you could see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll certainly try.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. I did the wrong thing there. See?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should show it to you, it's Z, for zigeuner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z, is zigeuner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gypsy. 2,801.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family 2,802?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't the first one. No, gosh, I don't know who was the first one. I think it was my dad and my mom, must have been the first ones. And then I think my sister, [GERMAN] is after me. I think she's 2,002. Yeah, of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think that's how I remembered that there were 8,000 people that night. Because they said the number went up to 8,000. That transport, whatever, came in there. You know what I mean? Do you know, did they have a separate numbering system for gypsies? Would the Jews that were after you have just continued the sequence? Or was it a separate sequence for gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they changed the thing in the front. There was no-- and the gypsies, they put the Z on. And they had the politicals there too, with the number. They just had the numbers on. And I think they was separated from the red [GERMAN] for the politics. And I think the black is for the [GERMAN] who don't want to work. And the green, I don't even know what that green was for. There was a green, they had a green. And then, of course, the Jewish star.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had black.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a shape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same [GERMAN] just a triangle thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it actually take to put the tattoo on? Was that a matter of minutes, or longer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you, well, they did it pretty fast. I will have to tell you, as you look at my number, I had kind of passed out a little bit there, for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept on finishing it up on it, OK. Because I don't think they did it then, what they do today. How they-- they have a different method today of putting tattoos on. Or to me, it felt like they just had regular ink pens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: It sure hurt. It surely hurt. And then, of course, you're frightened besides of all that. And of course, it got so musty in there, with all the people in there. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that was the bad part, too, just getting organized in there. You know what I mean? Because it was all new. Did you know what was going to happen as you went in line, before it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, when you're in there, then it comes word to word, you know what I mean? My dad, was the worst first thing. You know, he listened and talked, you know, while we were on the-- my dad and mother, [GERMAN], stay with your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he listened, you know. And he told us that we have to wait now. We've got to get tattooed and this and that. That's how we got the words, as young children, of the young ones. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this took all night. It took all that day. We got in there, all of us. Terrible, no food. No nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the babies, they tattooed on the arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Babies on the legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Babies on the legs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the babies come on the legs. They got the babies, because their little arms were too skinny. So they took the little flesh there, flesh part. But we didn't have a tiny little baby at that time. My brother, Fritz, was four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was done on the arm or the leg?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was done on the leg. On the leg. He was a little skinny guy. He turned out to be a little [GERMAN]. He ended up being a big guy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sick, mentally sick, real sick, you know what I mean? He didn't have no mama. He didn't have a dad. And really, if you talk to him about it, he doesn't remember that much, even, because of being so little, you know? So that's why I think it's a wonderful thing, with this testimony thing, that people don't forget this. And I think everybody should pay attention to this and take it serious. Because it would be a horrible thing if the human race would allow this again now. So tell me, so then you went through quarantine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the quarantine. I was out of the quarantine. And I told you, I went to see my dad. My dad didn't look good. I was awfully upset over that. And my mother said not to worry. Mothers do that, they don't want you-- whatever, this can't change it anyways. She didn't say that. But you know, she said, don't worry. You dad could lose a few pounds anyway. And I went back to this kitchen guy. And I just was sick, just crying away there. And after a week or so, I went there for that toothache. I was off again. That was my day off again. I went up there again, to visit them. And I got this toothache. And I told you, I lost my tooth. And this block leader took me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a room there, where they had their thing. And I could lay there. They had, like, a cot there. I could lay there till this was gone, my pain was gone. OK? So I was getting fevery. I had, like, a fever. And I thought it was from that thing I had, the fever. Well, I fell asleep there a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my mom came, with this block leader. And I remember he said, oh we got to take her in the sick block. We have to take her to the sick block. Yeah, can't keep her here. We can't put her in the kitchen. They knew, because they were experienced people, that was a prisoner also, about a prisoner who had been there for 12 years, some political prisoner, I imagine. I think that's what he was. He was a German guy, an elderly man. And that's all I remembered. And I remembered that they were telling me this. But I don't remember even getting into that sick block. All I remembered, I woke up. And I was on the top booth there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was, like, straw. They had some straw there, you know? And we had those red bowls. Red what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were red bowls where we used to get our food in, was like a porcelain bowl, was like a reddish-rustish color. That's the one we had. And there was tea. Like, it's supposed to be tea. I don't know. Once in a while they used to give you maybe a leaf. I don't know what they used for leaf teas. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, I seen the bowls laying there. And there was an old lady dipping in that bowl there, and wiping me. You know, with nothing on. You know what I mean? And there was two ladies up there. And one lady said, oh, I think she's coming to now. I heard that. Coming to, I think, what the heck's coming to? Where am I coming to from?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I kind of opened my eyes. And my whole teeth were sore. Everything I had in my mouth was hurting me. And of course, I looked at myself. And I was so dirty, I couldn't even read my number. This is why those people, those ladies were wiping me down with their tea. And one of the ladies says, oh, you're gonna be OK. You broke the crisis. You broke the crisis. I remember her saying that. And I was off again. And then the next day, it's gotten a little better. And then I came to a little bit. And then the ladies were telling me. I had typhoid. I was already there for three weeks. And of course, everybody in that block was sick. This is when I got the real education or what really was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was just dying. Everybody was dying. Everybody was dying. They all had white stuff. I remember seeing that white stuff on their faces. Because they had, like, the diarrhea, with the stomach typhoid. Anyway, it was a Polish woman who used to be a nurse. There was a lot of Polish people in medicine that was there. And she told me, says, you gonna be OK. You broke the crisis. And now you have to start eating, whatever you get, eat it. Or drink the tea, if it's good. Get that. Because my legs were like this. You know, I think I was 86 pounds. And I got out of that. I did, broke all that. And I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the other prisoners that took care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only prisoners. There was no real doctors there. The doctors, who were [INAUDIBLE] out there. But they didn't have much to work with. They had no medicines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used the old fashioned-- I don't what that white stuff was. It's like a chalk. That's for the diarrhea. And you know, the moaning and the groaning. In the back, in the back, in this bathroom-- a bathroom, it's a long open thing, it's like chicken-- you know, with a big hole. It's like a board. And everybody's mother, father, and everybody goes on that. Both sides, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, you adapt to those things. It's not that you like it or you don't like it. But you do have to go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to do those things. You cannot do without it. You know? And no matter where it is, if it's in the middle, you have to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the other side on this thing, that the visual-- there, I seen glass jars. They were big glass jars. And I couldn't believe it. I seen a couple of hats. It was a couple of feet, like, in alcohol. Bundles of that. They throw them right there in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you heard that plenty of times. Right? And piles, up there. That was in the back of the block now. OK? Back of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back of the camp, of the block. What we were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the sick bay? Or this is when you got back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick bay. No, all sick bay. And there was nobody allowed in there either, nobody. You don't know if your family all died out there, or who.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were strictly on your own. And it's not easy, coming out of this. You're so weak yourself. You know what I mean? Coming out of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you get a little selfish, for survival purposes, for some reason. Because if you concentrate on all that, you're never going to come out of it. There was a young girl there. She was under me. They threw her out, threw her down. That's when I remember that white face. It was a young girl. Because we didn't have any air. And that girl was still moving. And they already picked her and threw her in the back there. They already took her to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you get strong. You don't get weak from that stuff. You need to get strong. Or else we don't survive, OK? So anyway, and I seen those heads there in the back. I couldn't imagine, why would they do that? Now, it was another week or so. I got released.

speaker: interviewee
text: They checked you out. If you could march out of there, straight, you could get out of there. And they put you back in your block because they could put you back to work. They did not put me back in there, in my kitchen. I lost that job. See, in that kitchen, if nothing else, I could eat for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to sneak food? Or was it just there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sneak, no, sneak, but there it was. That I could. That's the only thing you could do. Hard work, it's the only thing you could do. So everybody else worked hard and didn't get to sneak the food. OK? Well, anyway, I got out of there. And of course, my mother was already on her way. My mother was sick too, very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she in the sick block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. She was very sick. Anyway, and she was happy to see me again. And I said, oh, Mama, I said, try and get out of this yourself. I said, there's so much misery in there. You won't make it. I said, Mama, you got to get up. You got to. And I told my sisters, let's help her. Let's do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We walked her down there, in the middle, in the block. We did this, we did that, just trying to get her a little exercise. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the meantime, there was a room, a room in that block, where you cut the bread. I told you that guy, that kapo there, whatever they called them, they had taken my dad. My dad helped them cut the bread. He helped them give out the portions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was, like, a little work room. It was not for the kapo himself, was this room. It was like a little work spot. It was across from you his room. Right in the beginning of the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a little bench in there where you could lay down if you wanted. It was wood. It was made out of wood. But you could lay down as well. Well, when my mom was not quite gone, this kapo said to my dad, the only thing I could do, you could lay her there in the daytime. But she has to stand up when they come and count. We were counted every day, in the morning and at night. And you have to stay up. If you can't stand up, you've got to go into the sick block. And that's the only way he kind of prolonged the whole thing a little bit. Finally, she couldn't stand up in the morning anymore. But in the meantime, I found about, I was telling my dad and my sisters about what I seen in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, you know what's happening here? There's a disease broke out for the children. They were swelling up in the ankles. The ankles were swelling up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the stomach was swelling. It was some kind of disease, but they didn't know enough about it. And they were studying that disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess the kids, they must have been dead or something. And they took those parts. But they had that all there, where they were working on that stuff. So we were exposed to all those things. You sit there doing your bit, here and there, those [INAUDIBLE] looking at you. You see? So now you just have to adapt to those things. You have to. Otherwise, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought if ever I go out again, I were not capable of loving anybody, not capable of trusting anybody. And I'll tell you, I never trust anybody, really, anyway. Authorities, I have no trust in authorities. Never did, even the police in here. Anything what has to do with authority, I am frightened. I don't know why. But I am frightened. I don't like a policeman stopping me for anything. Or if he did it for any reason, there's some kind of fear comes over me. And I'm 70 years old, and I never left. It's still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have this before you went into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, of course not. And it's not just me. Everyone, my family, everyone of us who survived and are still around, we all, there's some kind of a terrible fear for the authorities situation. Because probably we felt so betrayed and never grew out of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the barracks you stayed in. Were you with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The four sisters, yeah. Then, of course, my sister Mathilde got sick. And my sister Mathilde never came out of that sick block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't visit her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You could never get out of the sick block. We walked around up there a little bit. And we had a little whistle my dad taught us when we were children. If we were lost or something, if you heard that whistle, that was for us. And it was a family whistle. Nobody else-

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you do it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I sure can. I don't know if I still can do it right. I'll try. [WHISTLES HIGH-PITCH FOLLOWED BY LOW-PITCH]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was such so long-- and you know, I taught my children that. If I go in a shopping center, and I whistled, and my children automatic-- what, Ma? I'm here! Automatically. That's it. I taught my children the same thing. And that's the way my dad was. That whistle, we were kind of all recognized. How did I get to that whistle? You were trying to find your sister in the sick bay.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sick bay. Going around there a little bit, thought maybe she was capable of somehow, maybe, you could-- see, on top of those booths, there were no windows in there, of course. Now we're talking about Auschwitz. I'm not talking about Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about Auschwitz. We had [GERMAN], like in a bus. You know, you could ventilate the top? That's all there was in there. There was a front door and the back door. There was no windows in there. See? I'll tell you later in the story. You could look there, down a little bit. But

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody has to be a little bit more out. So I thought, maybe, if she was up there-- because I knew, I was in there. So I knew how it worked. I figured, if she's anywhere up there, if she's not on the bottom, she could hear it. And we kept on whistling. Never heard her. So the kapo, that kapo went and checked on her. It was Christmas, just before Christmas. And they told him she was on her way, that there was nothing they could do. For a matter of fact, my sister smoked. She was 21 years old those days. And he, somehow, gave her a cigarette or something, through this doctor, this Polish guy. You know, those kapos kind of worked together a little bit. And the kapo told him she really enjoyed that. So my sister died. And we never got to see her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1943? Christmas, 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right after Christmas. See, the first thing, my mother was only there three months, as you see on the death certificate. See, in the meantime, I was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my dad also got sick. This was the typhoid. This was so catchy. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody's mother had it. I had it again. There's the [GERMAN] typhus, there's the [GERMAN] typhus. There's different typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my dad started to get sickly too. And I thought, oh God, no, no, no, not my dad. You know, can't be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a man, I told you, like is. Now, my dad died. I forgot to tell you. The second time I was in there, the second time with that typhoid, my dad got sick when I was in there. My dad helped me. He says, you better get in there. He can't take you right now, right now you can't stand up and now you've got to go. Because those kapos had to stand straight for that situation. They were responsible for all of that stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, he cannot keep you anymore. He said, go into the sick block. You made it the last time. You're maybe going to do it again. You have to go. So I don't know how I got there. Because you go out. And you get such a high fever. And you go out, and you don't know what's happened to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came to that second time, it took me a little longer to get out of there. Took me a little longer. But when I came out, somehow, they made-- they knew there I was coming out, out there. And there they were, they were all sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what are you all so sad about? What's the matter? Papa just died. And then they didn't want to tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to scare me so much. Because I was just coming out myself. And they were telling me how, this was this big man. And that he went down. All he was laying in was those ribs. What you see, some of the pictures. And there was nothing uncommon there. All the people who died there died that way, of the starvation and the typhoid. And my dad was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my dad was gone. I was in the sick block. But he was in the [GERMAN] with the menfolks, you know what I mean? So now, where were we? Were we going to go back to something you were going to ask me? Well, I was just curious if you were staying in the barracks with your sisters? Or were you back now, with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, so there was two of them. OK? I was out. My other sister was out. My sister, Lena, also got the typhoid. See, you don't last but six, eight months, you're finished. Sooner or later, you're going to get it, no matter how quarantined you are. It's in the air. You're exposed to all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we don't have any special privileges. We got to wash block, just like them. They have the same. They had a wash thing where we washed our little thingies out with, you know what I mean? We didn't have any, just to keep half decently clean. But how much can you do? You don't have any soap. You don't got nothing. You can't do much there, OK? Only more. What job did you have after you were not working in the kitchen because of the typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out to the aussenkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You get up in the morning. You stand in line. You pick up that bowl of soup or bowl of tea, whatever they called it. And you got your [GERMAN], your quarter of a loaf, that thing. And you line up, and you're marching out to the camp. Now, we have done anything from building roads, building railroad tracks, fields, in the rain, in the snow. I got scars all over my feet, yet, from the wooden shoes. I got all kinds of scars on my feet. Wrapped the rags around it and all. And of course, on the aussenkommando, most of the time-- and we were on the aussenkommando outside-- we had woman guards. And I think woman guards, woman and woman is a terrible, terrible situation. There is no feeling between woman, when a woman is an authority over you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes, if you had a man guard, they were strict, but not quite as brutal as a woman. It's unbelievable, this could be. But that's the way it is. What were some of the things they did that was so terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, what did they do? Old ladies-- I remember, there was a woman. She was 64 years old, happened to be a German woman. It was on my block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I happened to look up there. And I said to myself, what the heck could a woman like that done? You know, why is she here for? So I had spoken to her. And I think I must have been already, close to almost a year there now. And she says she had four sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four sons in the German army, she told me. Had four sons. And first I got the telegram from the first one. And of course, she was very-- from losing the boy. And then the second one came. And then the third one came. Now, was the fourth telegram. And there was a woman mail's lady. The men were all in the front, I guess. And they had the woman, who delivered the mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she brought that last one, she made a little remark. She says, oh gosh. She said, I wish that Hitler was never born. My boys would be alive. You know what I mean? That's what she said.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that woman had turned her into the burgermeister. And that was against the Reich, whatever it was. And that woman was arrested. I mean, no [GERMAN] of any kind. They put her strictly to Auschwitz. And there, of course, she died. The woman died. Didn't live that long. She got that typhoid too.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of people died on the typhoid. And of course, that comes from starvation, from not having the right food, not being clean. You know what I mean? That's where the typhoid comes from. Was everyone in your block a political prisoner, an ethnic German? Or were you also mixed in with all the Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed. We were mixed. Jewish people, oh yeah, Jewish people. As a matter of fact, if there was anything wrong in the mixed block-- like we had Russian people there or Russian gypsies in there. There was Russian Jewish people in there. And I remember one night, they called a [GERMAN]. And I don't know how to explain that in English. The bell rings. And everybody has to get off the street, into the blocks. And the doors, the big gates-- there was those big brown doors-- closed, nobody out there, on the street. And all you hear is screaming going on, and trucks coming and going. And you don't know if they're going to come stop in front of your place, or next door, across the other side, or whatever. Anyway, I remember the men were all nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, well, listen. If they're going to come and get us, we are just going to-- if we just only take one or two with us. But we're not going to go willingly like that. OK? So they had their little get together. And everybody was kind of upset, and just waiting for that gate to open. There was no guns. Their boards, whatever they had, from those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were not going to just give it up. And after a certain amount of time, then at the end, when it was finished-- [IMITATES WHISTLE]. They blow that whistle again. And the [GERMAN] was [GERMAN] And of course, they opened up the door again. And kitty corner from us, there was a Russian, a Russian block. There was Russian gypsies in there. That's what it was, Russian gypsies, had emptied their whole block. And of course, you could hear the screaming on the way out there. They were screaming. It was four or five trucks going. Screaming as far as you could hear with your ears. They were taking them crematorium, to the gas chambers. Of course, we didn't know it at that time. But then the doors were opened. And there got to be talking around. They emptied that block, and they were all put to the chambers, gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
